lessons_learned: A critical success factor for phasing-out ODS was the development of effective partnerships with the private sector and the customs service.

The process from design to promulgation of any new policy or regulation at the national level is a complicated process that takes at minimum 1-2 years. Interventions in this field must be prioritized in order to develop realistic implementation schedules. In this regard, awareness-raising of political decision makers is of great value for promotion of political relevance of the regulations under development that could accelerate the national consultation and enactment processes.

The main barriers to adoption of low-GWP alternative technologies to HCFCs include lack of knowledge and local expertise in the new technology, higher cost of the technology, and limited or no availability of equipment and servicing tools in the local market.

The Covid-19 pandemic has put some constraints on the evaluative activities, in particular to conduct field mission for data collection and limited possibilities for triangulation of results obtained during desk reviews through observation and direct contact with project stakeholders and beneficiaries. In a normal situation, it is usually possible to organize all planned face-to-face meetings with project stakeholders and beneficiaries during a period of a standard one-week field mission of an international consultant. The remote conduct of this evaluation proved to be more demanding for timely organization of the planned virtual meetings as some stakeholders felt more freedom of choice that resulted in postponement of some interviews. Active involvement of UNDP MCO proved to be an important factor for organization of virtual meetings as the UN office can more easily convince national stakeholders and beneficiaries to adhere to the planned schedule of meetings with the evaluation team.
recommendations: The Government should provide the necessary support for submission of a GEF project on assistance for ratification and implementation of the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol in Azerbaijan.
The Government should consider adoption of necessary steps towards regulations supporting establishment of an official certification of Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning (RAC) service technicians.
The Government with cooperation of the main companies in the RAC sector should encourage establishment of a national RAC Association.

The Climate Change and Ozone Centre within the Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources (MENR) should approach the UNEP ECA network for access to available standards and codes of good practice in the RAC sector.

The Climate Change and Ozone Centre within the MENR should consider development of outreach activities aiming at the end-users of RAC equipment to explain risks and disadvantages of engagements with the informal servicing sub-sector.
In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include sensitisation of the highest level of Government officials about the benefits of prioritization of legislative updates for HFC control.
In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include activities on demonstration of economic benefits of refrigerant recycling and reuse.
In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should include importers and distributors of HFC refrigerants and equipment in training and awareness raising activities.

In the future projects for HFC phase-down, UNIDO should ensure that indicators and targets are included in the project results framework for measurement and reporting on effectiveness of the national system for ODS control, e.g. indicators and targets for measurement of adherence to good practices in RAC servicing and for reporting of actual quantities of refrigerant recovery, re-use, and reclamation.

For future projects on HFC control, UNIDO should use the existing LTAs and eventually develop additional LTAs for procurement of equipment items of recurrent demand in order to reduce workload on administration of the procurement events and the time needed for acquisition of procured items.

For future GEF projects on ODS control, UNIDO should identify national partners in order to comply with the requirement for separation of the implementation and execution functions for the projects as stipulated in the GEF Updated Policy on Minimum Fiduciary Standards.

In preparation of the project on HFC control, UNIDO should pay due attention to proper formulation of the indicators and targets in the project results framework in order to facilitate M&E of the project.

UNIDO Management should ensure that for projects on ODS control a management response to the recommendations of Mid-term Reviews is prepared similar to the provisions of the UNIDO Evaluation Policy for independent evaluations.
executive_summary: This terminal evaluation assessed UNIDO's project supporting Azerbaijan's phase-out of hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) under the Montreal Protocol. The project aimed to eliminate HCFC consumption through technical assistance, capacity building, and policy support. Key interventions included establishing a licensing and quota system, training customs officials and refrigeration technicians, supporting end-of-life refrigerant management, and raising public awareness. The evaluation found the project highly relevant to national environmental commitments and international obligations. Implementation faced significant delays due to procurement challenges, government restructuring, and COVID-19 disruptions. Despite these obstacles, the project achieved its core objective of HCFC phase-out, with Azerbaijan meeting Montreal Protocol compliance targets. The evaluation rated effectiveness as satisfactory, noting successful capacity development of the National Ozone Unit and improved monitoring systems. Sustainability concerns were raised regarding the long-term viability of the refrigerant recovery and recycling system due to limited economic incentives. The project demonstrated strong country ownership through government co-financing and institutional integration. Recommendations emphasized strengthening enforcement mechanisms, enhancing regional cooperation, and ensuring continued support for the transition to climate-friendly alternatives beyond HCFCs.
thematic_justification: The project directly addresses ozone-depleting substances (HCFCs) phase-out under the Montreal Protocol through regulatory frameworks, technical capacity building, and substance management systems, while also contributing to climate mitigation given HCFCs' high global warming potential.

sdg_mapping.13: The project contributes to climate action by phasing out HCFCs which are both ozone-depleting substances and potent greenhouse gases with high global warming potential, thereby reducing climate impacts while addressing ozone layer protection.

context.title: Independent Terminal Evaluation: Initiation of the HCFC Phase Out in the Republic of Azerbaijan
